summary: I was born in Eagle Rock in 1915, in a house my parents built on a hilltop overlooking the valley. My family moved to southern California around 1910 and chose Eagle Rock as the place to settle down. It was a special place for us, and I was actually born right there in the house, not in a hospital.

My parents relocated to southern California in 1910 after my father retired from his business ventures in the Pacific Northwest. They settled in Eagle Rock, which is part of southern California, and built a home there. It was a fresh start for the family after years of working in the timber country up north.

Glendale was our closest supply point when we lived in Eagle Rock. Although I was born in our home, Glendale was the nearest town where we got our supplies and necessities. It was an important nearby community for us during those early years.

Before moving to southern California, my father ran a successful general merchandise chain in the Pacific Northwest. He and his brothers operated stores that served the timber country, supplying everything needed by the workers there. That region included several towns in Washington where our business was well known.

Aberdeen was one of the key towns in the Pacific Northwest where my father and his brothers had their Veysey Brothers stores. These stores supplied the logging communities and were an important part of our family’s business operations. I remember hearing about the timber work and the bustling activity in places like Aberdeen.

Hoquiam was another town in Washington where my father’s general merchandise stores were located. Along with Aberdeen and other nearby towns, Hoquiam was part of the timber country that our family served. The stores there helped support the logging workers and their families.

Montesano was one of the smaller towns where my father and his brothers operated their Veysey Brothers stores. It was part of the network of communities in the Pacific Northwest timber region that relied on our family’s merchandise business. These towns were closely connected through the logging industry.

Elma was included among the Washington towns where my father’s stores did business. Like Aberdeen, Hoquiam, and Montesano, Elma was part of the timber country that our family served with general merchandise. These communities were central to my father’s career before he retired and moved us to California.
